title: ER Diagram Notation Reference Table
examTip: A strong entity is drawn with a single rectangle, whereas a weak entity has a double rectangle. Its identifying relationship is drawn as a double diamond. A key attribute is underlined, a multi-valued attribute is a double ellipse, and a derived attribute is a dashed ellipse.

rows.value: Represents a principal, independent object type
Dependent on a strong identifying entity set
Represents an association between entity sets
Links a weak entity to its owner entity set
Represents a basic property of an entity
Can store multiple values (e.g., Phone Number)
Computed from other attributes (e.g., Age from DOB)

theory:
The Entity-Relationship (ER) model uses a standardized set of graphical symbols to model conceptual schemas. In Mumbai University and major database textbooks (e.g., Silberschatz, Korth, Sudarshan), the standard Chen notation is used.

1. Entities:
- Strong (Regular) Entity: An object with a distinct, independent existence. Drawn as a single rectangle.
- Weak Entity: An entity that does not possess a primary key and depends on a strong identifying entity set. Drawn as a double rectangle. It must have a partial key (discriminator) represented with a dashed underline.

2. Relationships:
- Regular Relationship: Connects independent entity sets. Drawn as a diamond.
- Identifying Relationship: Connects a weak entity set to its strong owner entity set. Drawn as a double diamond.
- Participation: Total participation is shown by double lines connecting the entity to the relationship; partial participation is shown by a single line.

3. Attributes:
- Base Attribute: A property or characteristic of an entity. Drawn as an ellipse.
- Key Attribute: The primary key of the entity, uniquely identifying each instance. Represented by an ellipse containing underlined text.
- Multivalued Attribute: An attribute that can have more than one value for a single entity (e.g., phone numbers, skillsets). Represented by a double ellipse.
- Derived Attribute: An attribute whose value is computed dynamically from other stored attributes (e.g., calculating Age from Date of Birth). Drawn as a dashed ellipse.
- Composite Attribute: An attribute divided into sub-attributes (e.g., Name divided into First Name, Last Name). Drawn as branches extending from the parent ellipse.
keyPoints: Rectangles represent Entities (single = strong, double = weak)
Diamonds represent Relationships (single = regular, double = identifying for weak entities)
Ellipses represent Attributes (single = base, underlined = key, double = multivalued, dashed = derived)
Chen's notation is the standard representation for conceptual database design in exams
Participation: single line indicates partial participation, double line indicates total participation
Cardinality constraints are labeled on lines (e.g., 1:1, 1:N, M:N or using arrows)
useCases: Logical Database Design: Creating ER diagrams before mapping to relational database tables
Database Documentation: Presenting logical schemas to application developers and stakeholders
Academic Exams: Drawing notations to model student-course, hospital, or library management systems
